title: Funding-block claims can be replayed and reorder payouts because the round snapshot is mutable within the same block
claim: `initiateRound()` snapshots the round at `block.number`, but `processClaim()` still accepts a claim when `lastClaimedFor == currentRound.fundedBlock`. Reward staking then writes new checkpoints at that same block, and the checkpoint lookup returns the latest value for an equal block number. This makes the supposed funding-block snapshot mutable during the funded block, so same-block replays and intra-block claim ordering change later reward calculations.
impact: A service provider can bundle `initiateRound()` with multiple same-block `claimRewards()` calls and mint more rewards than intended for that round because `currentRound.totalClaimedInRound` is tracked but never capped. Even without duplicate self-claims, any claims mined earlier in the funded block rewrite the balances later claims read, creating MEV-sensitive overpayment for early claimers and underpayment for later ones.
paths: Bundle `initiateRound()` and `claimRewards(sp)` twice in the same block; the second call still passes because equality with `fundedBlock` is allowed.
Mine multiple service-provider claims in the funding block so earlier claims rewrite `totalStakedAt(fundedBlock)` and per-provider stake at that same block before later claims execute.

title: Matured stake-removal actions remain executable during a pending-claim window, letting providers claim against stake they already removed
claim: The protocol blocks creating new withdrawal/removal requests while a claim is pending, but it does not block executing already-matured `decreaseStake()` or `removeDelegator()` during that same claim window. `ClaimsManager.processClaim()` mints rewards from the funding-block snapshot, while `DelegateManager` distributes those rewards using the current delegator set and current balances. A provider can therefore remove stake after the snapshot but before claiming, while still claiming rewards attributable to the pre-removal stake.
impact: A service provider can queue a delegator removal or stake decrease before a round, wait for the next round to start, execute the matured removal after the snapshot, and then claim rewards as if the removed stake were still backing the provider. This can divert rewards that should have gone to the removed delegator, and it also lets the provider reduce slashable backing after the round snapshot without giving up the corresponding round rewards.
paths: Queue `requestRemoveDelegator(sp, delegator)` before expiry, wait for the next round to start, call `removeDelegator(sp, delegator)`, then call `claimRewards(sp)`.
Queue `requestDecreaseStake()` before a round, let it mature, then after `initiateRound()` but before claiming call `decreaseStake()` and finally `claimRewards(sp)`.

title: Dust-stake accounts can fill every governance proposal slot and repeatedly delay protocol actions
claim: Proposal submission only requires non-zero active stake and imposes no proposer threshold, bond, cooldown, or per-address cap, while `maxInProgressProposals` globally caps how many proposals may exist at once. This allows an address with trivial stake to occupy all proposal slots with spam proposals for full voting windows.
impact: Governance liveness can be griefed cheaply: a dust staker can keep all proposal slots full and force legitimate proposals to wait for voting periods and execution delays to elapse, delaying upgrades or emergency actions unless a guardian actively intervenes to veto the spam.
paths: Acquire minimal active stake, submit proposals until `inProgressProposals.length == maxInProgressProposals`, and resubmit new proposals as slots free up.

title: A guardian proposal submitted when total stake is zero can become permanently unevaluable and block new proposals
claim: The guardian may submit proposals even when no stake exists, but `_quorumMet()` divides by `stakingContract.totalStakedAt(proposal.submissionBlockNumber)` without handling the zero-stake case. If a proposal is submitted at a block with zero total stake, `evaluateProposalOutcome()` reverts on quorum calculation and never removes the proposal from `inProgressProposals`.
impact: After the proposal becomes evaluatable, every evaluation attempt reverts and `inProgressProposalsAreUpToDate()` stays false, preventing submission of new proposals. Governance liveness then depends on the guardian successfully vetoing the stuck proposal; if the guardian is unavailable or the bad proposal was submitted by the guardian itself, proposal creation can be frozen indefinitely.
paths: With zero total stake, the guardian calls `submitProposal(...)`; once voting and execution delay end, `evaluateProposalOutcome()` reverts on division by zero and the proposal remains in progress.

title: Governance proposal code-hash pinning does not detect proxy implementation upgrades
claim: Governance stores `extcodehash(targetContractAddress)` when a proposal is submitted and compares it again at evaluation, but proposals are executed against registry proxy contracts. Upgrading a proxy's implementation changes behavior while leaving the proxy bytecode and therefore its `extcodehash` unchanged, so the `TargetContractCodeHashChanged` safeguard does not actually pin proposal semantics for proxied targets.
impact: A proposal can be reviewed and approved against one implementation, then executed against materially different logic if the target proxy's implementation is upgraded before evaluation. This undermines the integrity check intended to ensure the voted-on action is the action ultimately executed.
paths: Submit a proposal whose registry key resolves to a proxy, upgrade that proxy's implementation before proposal evaluation, then evaluate the original proposal; the code-hash check still passes because the proxy shell bytecode is unchanged.

title: Claims use the mutable global `fundingAmount` instead of the round's snapshotted `fundedAmount`
claim: Although each round snapshots `currentRound.fundedAmount`, `processClaim()` computes rewards from the live `fundingAmount` variable instead of the round snapshot. If governance changes `fundingAmount` after a round starts but before all claims are processed, later claims in the same round use a different effective round budget than earlier claims.
impact: Payouts within a single funding round become mutable and order-dependent around `updateFundingAmount()`. Earlier claimers can be paid using one budget while later claimers are paid using another, breaking round immutability and enabling intra-round overpayment or underpayment relative to the announced funded amount.
paths: Start round `R`, allow some providers to claim, call `updateFundingAmount(newValue)`, then let remaining providers claim in the same round; claims within `R` use different reward budgets.

title: Permissionless `claimRewards` can burn a provider's entire round by finalizing a zero-value claim during temporary ineligibility
claim: `DelegateManager.claimRewards()` is callable by any address for any service provider. When `ClaimsManager.processClaim()` finds the provider out of bounds or computes zero rewards, it still updates claim history and returns 0. Because later claims in that round are blocked once `lastClaimedFor(_sp)` exceeds `currentRound.fundedBlock`, a third party can permanently consume the round for a provider by calling `claimRewards(_sp)` while the provider is only temporarily ineligible.
impact: An attacker can deny a service provider and its delegators an entire round of inflation rewards without stealing them, simply by timing a permissionless claim during a temporary out-of-bounds or fully locked state. Repeating this whenever such windows arise creates recurring economic grief and reward loss.
paths: Wait until after a round starts while a provider is temporarily out of bounds or all active stake is locked, then call `claimRewards(sp)` from any address; `processClaim()` records a zero-value claim.
After the provider restores stake bounds or unlocks stake later in the round, a legitimate claim for that round fails because claim history already marks the round as processed.
